sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “For My Own Name’s Sake I Delay My Wrath”

heb.paragraph: "For my own name’s sake (shēm) I delay my wrath; for the sake of my praise I hold it back from you, so as not to destroy you completely" (v.9). God recounts Israel’s stubbornness: "I knew how treacherous you were; you were called a rebel from birth" (v.8). He predicted events in advance specifically because Israel would credit idols otherwise (vv.3–5). "I have refined you, though not as silver; I have tested you in the furnace of affliction" (v.10). "For my own sake, for my own sake, I do this. How can I let myself be defamed?" (v.11). God’s motivation: his own name, his own glory, his own reputation. Not Israel’s merit.

notes.note: "I foretold the former things long ago. I told you before they happened so that you could not say, ‘My idol did them.’" Prophecy as anti-idolatry: God predicts to prevent misattribution.

"I have refined you in the furnace of affliction. For my own sake, for my own sake, I do this." Exile as furnace. Salvation for God’s sake — doubled for emphasis. This is the theology of Ezekiel 36:22 too.

sections.header: Verses 12–22 — “Go Out from Babylon! Flee from the Chaldeans!”

heb.paragraph: "Leave Babylon, flee (ṣəʾū) from the Babylonians! Announce this with shouts of joy and proclaim it. Send it out to the ends of the earth; say, ‘The LORD has redeemed his servant Jacob!’" (v.20). "I am the first and I am the last" (v.12) — repeated from 44:6. "My own hand laid the foundations of the earth, and my right hand spread out the heavens" (v.13). God called Cyrus; God will fulfil his purpose against Babylon (v.15). "Come near me and listen: from the first announcement I have not spoken in secret; at the time it happens, I am there" (v.16). Then the lament: "If only you had paid attention to my commands, your peace would have been like a river, your well-being like the waves of the sea" (v.18). "There is no peace for the wicked" (v.22) — the refrain that divides Second Isaiah into three sections (48:22; 57:21; ending implied at 66:24).

notes.note: "From the first announcement I have not spoken in secret." God’s revelation is public, historical, verifiable. Not whispered in mystery temples but declared openly.
"If only you had paid attention! Your peace would have been like a river." The conditional lament: God imagines what could have been. River-peace and wave-wellbeing — the loss is immeasurable.

"Leave Babylon! Announce it with shouts of joy! The LORD has redeemed his servant Jacob!" The exodus command: leave now. Shout while you go. But: "There is no peace for the wicked." The section-divider: peace for the redeemed, none for the wicked.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYou have neither heard nor understood; from of old your ears have not been open. Well do I know how treacherous you are; you were called a rebel from birth.ESVYou have never heard, you have never known, from of old your ear has not been opened. For I knew that you would surely deal treacherously, and that from before birth you were called a rebel.NIV“Listen to this, you descendants of Jacob, you who are called by the name of Israel and come from the line of Judah, you who take oaths in the name of the Lord and invoke the God of Israel— but not in truth or righteousness—ESVHear this, O house of Jacob, who are called by the name of Israel, and who came from the waters of Judah, who swear by the name of the LORD and confess the God of Israel, but not in truth or right.

rec.text: ""I foretold the former things long ago. I told you before they happened so that you could not say, ‘My idol did them.’" Prophecy as anti-idolatry: God predicts to prevent misattribution."

rows.label: Refined in Affliction — 48:1–11
rows.text: "The FURNACE OF AFFLICTION." "For MY OWN SAKE, for my own sake, I do this." God’s name-driven salvation.

hebtext.note: "For my own name’s sake (shēm) I delay my wrath; for the sake of my praise I hold it back from you, so as not to destroy you completely" (v.9). God recounts Israel’s stubbornness: "I knew how treacherous you were; you were called a rebel from birth" (v.8). He predicted events in advance specifically because Israel would credit idols otherwise (vv.3–5). "I have refined you, though not as silver; I have tested you in the furnace of affliction" (v.10). "For my own sake, for my own sake, I do this. How can I let myself be defamed?" (v.11). God’s motivation: his own name, his own glory, his own reputation. Not Israel’s merit.

"Leave Babylon, flee (ṣəʾū) from the Babylonians! Announce this with shouts of joy and proclaim it. Send it out to the ends of the earth; say, ‘The LORD has redeemed his servant Jacob!’" (v.20). "I am the first and I am the last" (v.12) — repeated from 44:6. "My own hand laid the foundations of the earth, and my right hand spread out the heavens" (v.13). God called Cyrus; God will fulfil his purpose against Babylon (v.15). "Come near me and listen: from the first announcement I have not spoken in secret; at the time it happens, I am there" (v.16). Then the lament: "If only you had paid attention to my commands, your peace would have been like a river, your well-being like the waves of the sea" (v.18). "There is no peace for the wicked" (v.22) — the refrain that divides Second Isaiah into three sections (48:22; 57:21; ending implied at 66:24).

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: for my own sake. First and Last again. The exodus-from-Babylon command. "No peace for the wicked" divides Second Isaiah.
